coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of lanthanum doped carbon dots for detection of mercury ion, multi-color imaging of cells and tissue, and bacteriostasis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ming
Wang, Wentao
Yuan, Ping
Chi, Cheng
Zhang, Jun
Zhou, Ninglin
coredata.dc:description: This work describes a hydrothermal method for the preparation of multi-functional carbon quantum dots of the type La-CQDs. The La-CQDs are 4.3±0.3nm in diameter and have a good dispersion and exhibit excellent emission property and high stability, as well as excitation-dependent emission behavior with typical excitation/emission peaks at 350/460nm. They can also be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the quenchometric determination of mercury ion in aqueous solution with a detection limit of 0.1µM. The particles are readily internalized by liver epithelial HepG2 cells and enable multi-color fluorescence imaging by displaying green and red emission if excited with 488 and 543nm light, respectively. Intravenous injection of the quantum dots in nude mice, in turn, enabled tissue imaging. The La-CQDs possess abundant phosphate, hydroxyl, pyridinic, pyridine-like and pyrrole-like nitrogen groups. Therefore, they can exhibit strong fluorescence, improve the accuracy as an optical monitoring code both in vitro and in vivo, and enhance blood compatibility. In vitro investigations show that La-CQDs do not exhibit significant apoptotic effects on NIH3T3 cells even at a high concentration (2.5mg/mL) while they exhibit improved antibactericidal capacity of the La-CQDs against gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria was also observed. No apparent pathological damages are observed in all organs during the 30-day period, which suggests that La-CQDs do not produce detectable long-term toxicological responses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Antibactericidal
Blood compatibility
Toxicological responses

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in cancer cells
In vitro and vivo cytotoxicity study of La-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the possible mechanism for the formation and the application of La-CQDs.

(A) TEM of the synthesized La-CQDs. The inset shows the histogram of particle size distribution and HRTEM images. (B) XPS of La-CQDs and N,P-CQDs. (C) EDS of La-CQDs and N,P-CQDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of ATP, La-CQDs, and N,P-CQDs.

(A) TGA curves measured for La-CQDs and N,P-CQDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of ATP, La-CQDs, and N,P-CQDs. Inset: Photographs of the aqueous La-CQDs (a) and N,P-CQDs (b) under UV light (365nm). (C) Normalized photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of La-CQDs and N,P-CQDs. (D) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of La-CQDs.

(A) Relative PL intensity of La-CQDs aqueous solution with the increase of pH from 2 to 13. Inset: Photographs of the aqueous La-CQDs at different pH under daylight and UV light (365nm). (B) Relative fluorescence intensity at ex=350nm of aqueous La-CQDs solution in the presence of 25μM of various metal ions. Inset: Photographs of the aqueous La-CQDs containing 25μM of various metal ions under daylight and UV light (365nm). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of La-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0 to 50μM), excite 350nm. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of La-CQDs in the absence or presence of Hg2+ (50μM). The excitation wavelength is 350nm.

Comparison of 2D (A–C) and 3D (D–F) z-stack images of 3D cultured HepG2 cells cultivated with La-CQDs: (A, D) 488/500nm (green), (B, E) 543/650nm (red), (C) overlay of A and B, (F) overlay of D and E, Scale bar: 50μm. (G) In vivo fluorescence images after tail vein injection of La-CQDs in nude mice. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence imaging of A549 cells incubated with La-CQDs (A) in the presence of Hg2+ solution (25μM) (B).
SEM images of E. coli (A) and S. aureus (B) exposed to PVA composite films. MTT result of E. coli and S. aureus cultured on three different materials for 24h. (Number of experiments: 3).

(A) Cell toxicity test results of ATP, N,P-CQDs, and La-CQDs at different concentrations for 24h. (B) Flow cytometry analysis of NIH3T3 cells before and after the incubation with ATP (a), N,P-CQDs (b) and La-CQDs (c). (C) The samples with different concentrations of hemolysis rate. The insets: Photograph of the supernatant from positive control (a, water), negative control (b, PBS), ATP (c, 100µg/ml), N,P-CQDs (b, 100µg/ml) and La-CQDs (d, 100µg/ml) suspensions. (D) Body weight of the La-CQDs-treated mice during 30days. (E) Biodistribution of P in major organs of mice without or with La-CQDs injection at different time points postinjection. (F) Pathological evaluation (hematoxylin and eosin stained images) obtained from the liver, spleen, kidney, heart, and lung of the La-CQDs-treated mice at 1, 10 and 30days postinjection. (Number of experiments: 3).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of lanthanum doped carbon dots for detection of mercury ion, multi-color imaging of cells and tissue, and bacteriostasis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials, Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials
Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Zhou Ninglin Advanced Materials Technology Company Limited, Nanjing 211505, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing Zhou Ninglin Advanced Materials Technology Company Limited

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Molecular and Medical Biotechnology, College of Life Sciences, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Molecular and Medical Biotechnology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes a hydrothermal method for the preparation of multi-functional carbon quantum dots of the type La-CQDs. The La-CQDs are 4.3±0.3nm in diameter and have a good dispersion and exhibit excellent emission property and high stability, as well as excitation-dependent emission behavior with typical excitation/emission peaks at 350/460nm. They can also be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the quenchometric determination of mercury ion in aqueous solution with a detection limit of 0.1µM. The particles are readily internalized by liver epithelial HepG2 cells and enable multi-color fluorescence imaging by displaying green and red emission if excited with 488 and 543nm light, respectively. Intravenous injection of the quantum dots in nude mice, in turn, enabled tissue imaging. The La-CQDs possess abundant phosphate, hydroxyl, pyridinic, pyridine-like and pyrrole-like nitrogen groups. Therefore, they can exhibit strong fluorescence, improve the accuracy as an optical monitoring code both in vitro and in vivo, and enhance blood compatibility. In vitro investigations show that La-CQDs do not exhibit significant apoptotic effects on NIH3T3 cells even at a high concentration (2.5mg/mL) while they exhibit improved antibactericidal capacity of the La-CQDs against gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria was also observed. No apparent pathological damages are observed in all organs during the 30-day period, which suggests that La-CQDs do not produce detectable long-term toxicological responses.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional nanomaterials have attracted extensive attentions because of their application prospects in the field of biomedicine [1–6]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of semiconductor nanoparticles, which can be used for bioimaging, disease detection, drug delivery, and photovoltaic devices [7–11]. However, the toxicity and potential environmental toxicity of quantum dots have limited its application [12–15]. Therefore, it is important to find the excellent nanomaterials for biological markers, disease detection, and drug delivery. CQDs emerged as a new kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial [16]. As skeleton elements of all organisms, carbon very attractive due to its low toxicity and good biocompatibility. CQDs can be swallowed by cells, and enter cell interior without affecting nucleus. CQDs can also interact with DNA, suggesting that they possess high sensitivity and selectivity for DNA [17,18]. CQDs, as a new type of fluorescent probes for metal ions, are easily quenched by electron acceptor in solution, which can effectively detect metal ions and biomolecules in solution [19,20]. Liu’s group prepared magnesium and nitrogen co-doped CQDs, which were used as a fluorescent switch for sensitive and selective sensing of mercury ions (Hg2+) and cysteine [21]. As a biological fluorescent probes, some researchers CQDs were also used as the carrier import fluorescent dyes for cell imaging studies [22,23]. Wang’s group succeeded in injecting hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs and used them to measure the pH of plant cells due to the fluorescent calibrated by using sensitivity of the CQDs on pH [24].

Mercury ion is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions and a dangerous pollutants [25–27]. Mercury ion may cause damage to brain, nervous system, kidney, heart and endocrine system [28]. Therefore, it is important to develop effective analytical methods for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ion [29]. So far, due to the fluorescent properties of the CQDs, particularly the low emission efficiency, numerous approaches have been designed for the detection of mercury ions [30]. Yang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs using adipic acid and triammonium citrate as carbon and nitrogen sources for the quantitative determination and intracellular imaging of mercury ion [31]. Fluorescent CQDs prepared from EDTA was also used to assay mercury ions in aqueous solutions. It is well known that a high quantum yield (QY) is the essential factor for bioimaging. Unfortunately, the N,P-doped CQDs synthesized from these cheap and abundant precursors did not achieve high QY preventing their practical use. Therefore, there is still room to search for a low-cost precursor to develop a rapid synthesis of highly photoluminescent CQDs on a large scale. Improving the QY of CQDs is still a big challenge for their wide applications.

Antibacterial materials are widely used in our daily life due to their friendliness for the public health protection [32,33]. For instance, the compounds of metal ion and quaternary ammonium have been shown to effectively prevent the microbes and bacteria from surface adhesion and growth. However, these materials often cause drug resistance and environment pollution [34]. Wound healing is usually facilitated by the use of a wound dressing that can be readily applied to cover the wound bed and avoid bacterial infection [35], Hence, the preparation of antibacterial materialis of great importance. Meanwhile, a growing problem of antibiotic resistance in the world has now rekindled the interest in antibacterial packaging, which was generally investigated as a response to consumer demands or industrial production trends toward tenderly preserved, fresh, tasty and convenient food products with extended shelf-life and controlled quality [35]. Azlin-Hasim’s group developed silver nanodot surfaces exhibited good antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and potentially can potentially be used in antimicrobial packaging application [36]. Wang’s group prepared Nisin–chitosan/poly(vinyl alcohol) antibacterial films by coating method, which achieved well controlled release and better antimicrobial activity against S. aureus [37]. Nanoantimicrobial agents have emerged in the market, and have excellent antibacterial effect. However, it is unable to find the antibacterial agent that can label the bacteria and restrain the bacteria. We have proved to be well controlled antimicrobial and bacteria imaging effect by adjusting the concentration of CQDs.

In this work, multifunctional La-CQDs with bright photoluminescence were prepared from Adenosine disodium triphosphate (ATP) and LaCl3·2H2O by solvothermal treatment under mild temperature conditions. In this approach, La-CQDs with an average size of 4.3±0.34nm exhibited bright blue luminescence, short fluorescence lifetime, pH-sensitivity, and high stability. Remarkably, the prepared La-CQDs showed good stability in high-salt conditions and can be used to detect mercury ions in an aqueous solution. The characteristics of excitation wavelength-dependent tunable photoluminescence improved the accuracy of La-CQDs as an optical code both in vitro and in vivo. La-CQDs without any further surface modification can be functionalized with other active substances for specific applications in the biomedical field and environmental field applications.

Adenosine disodium triphosphate (ATP) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate (99%) and LaCl3·2H2O was supplied by Aladdin. Hg2+ stock solution (0.1mg/ml) were prepared by dissolving HgCl2 in purified water obtained through a water purification system (Youpu UPT-II-10T, Beijing). All the other chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. Different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were prepared by diluting standard stock solutions. Other metal salts used in this work include NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, 3CdSO4·8H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CuSO4·5H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, AgNO3, and LaCl3·2H2O.

Adult whole blood was provided by Jiangsu blood center. Mouse embryonic fibroblast cells (NIH3T3), human liver carcinoma cells (HepG2) and adenocarcinomic alveolar basal epithelial cells (A549) were provided by the Drum Tower Hospital of Jiangsu Province; S. aureus and E. coli, provided by the Jiangsu Provincial Center for Disease Control and prevention. High glucose DMEM (HyClone) were purchased from Thermo Fisher (USA). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), penicillin, and streptomycin were purchased from Hyclone Co., Ltd (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from SunShine Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China).

The photoluminescent (PL) spectra were recorded using molecular fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, varian, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was recorded using the Time-resolved Fluorescence Life Time (FM-4P-TCSPC, Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a PerkinElmer Frontier spectrometer, and samples were prepared in KBr pellets. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D/Max 2500V/PC diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using UV absorption spectrophotometer (Cary-50, varian, USA). The surface composition and element analysis of the samples were recorded using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo-Fisher, USA, www.thermofisher.com). The morphologies of the samples were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescent images of cells were acquired by laser scanning confocal microscope (TI-E-A1R, Nikon, Japan). Fluorescent scans were performed mainline using a Maestro 2 Multispectral Small-animal Imaging System. Detail of characterization results was provided in Electronic Supplement Material.

ATP (1.0g) and LaCl3·2H2O (1.5g) was dissolved in 25ml deionized water to form a transparent solution. After stirring for 30min, the mixture was sealed into a 50ml Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 160°C for 8h. After cooling to room temperature, a homogeneous solution, named La-CQDs, was obtained. To further purify La-CQDs, the solution was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min to remove small volume of liquid and filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove large or agglomerated particles. The purification of the La-CQDs was conducted through a dialysis tube (1000Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 24h in dark. The obtained La-CQDs solution were dried to a powder by vacuum freeze-drying machine and stored at 4°C prior to use. N,P-CQDs were synthesized by a method similar to the preparation of N,P-CQDs, but without LaCl3·2H2O.

Cells were seeded at a density of 1×105 cells/well in a 35-mm Petri dish with DMEM containing 10% FBS, 1% penicillin and streptomycin, and incubated at 37°C for 24h. Next, culture medium was replaced with fresh medium (DMEM containing 0.5mg/ml of La-CQDs) after removing obsolete DMEM containing 10% FBS, 1% penicillin and streptomycin mixed solution. The A549 cells were passed and plated on polylysine-coated cell culture glass slides inside the 35-mm culture dishes and allowed to adhere for 24h. Subsequently, the culture medium was replaced with 2.5ml fresh medium containing La-CQDs (10μg/ml). Then the cells were firstly incubated for 24h and then incubated with 25μM Hg2+ for another 0.5h at 37°C. The cells were imaged again after washing three times with PBS.

A FITC Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit (BD company, US) was used to detect apoptotic and necrotic cells. The manufacturer’s instructions were strictly followed. Briefly, NIH3T3 cells in the 37°C incubator containing 5% CO2 were cultured for 72h. The ATP, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs with 2.5 mg/ml were introduced to the cells, which were incubated for another 24h. Then, the cells were washed twice with PBS and resuspended in the buffer solution to 1×106 cells/ml. The above-mentioned cell solution (100μl), annexin V-FITC (5μl), and propidium iodide (PI, 5μl) were successively added to a tube. After incubation for 15min in the dark at room temperature, 400μl of PBS was added to dilute the stained HepG2 cells, and fluorescence activated cell sorting was applied to analyze the apoptotically or necrotically cell.

Female C57BL/6 mice were purchased, fed, and processed based on the protocols approved by the Institute of Radiation Medicine, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences (IRM, CAMS). The mice were randomly divided into four groups: control group, 1day treatment group, 10days treatment group, and 30days treatment group, respectively. An amount of 200μl of the La-CQDs solution (1.5mg/ml) was injected into the mice via tail vein. Body weight was recorded, and the behavioral changes of mice were assessed every day. During the autopsy, heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were collected for pathological analyses. The phosphorus content was measured by an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Agilent 7500 CE, Germany).

Nowadays, there have been many articles reporting N,P-CQDs. Our team through a rapid hydrothermal prepared La-CQDs containing N, P and antibacterial activity of lanthanum element through a rapidhydrothermal method. Remarkably, ATP, as a nitrogen and phosphorus-rich compound, is the direct source of almost all the energy needed processes in vivo. It is found that lanthanum(III) ion (La3+) can promote the formation of osteoclasts and bone resorption. In addition, La3+ and its complex can effectively inhibit the growth of a variety of cancer cells, and have potential anti-tumor application. As shown in Fig. 1 , the ATP ionizes into negative ions after it is dissolved in water, then La3+ and ATP are coordinated with each other to form ATP crosslinking. La3+ has a larger radius and higher coordination number than other transition metals. Finally, with the increase of temperature, the amino and hydroxyl groups may interact with phosphoric acid groups to undergo dehydration, condensation, and carbonization [38]. Simultaneously, the particle size of La-CQDs is getting smaller and smaller as the reaction goes on (Fig. S1). The surface of lattice structures of La-CQDs possesses abundant phosphate, hydroxy, carboxyl and amino groups that endowed the La-CQDs with attractive photoluminescence and excellent water dispersibility. These characteristics endowed La-CQDs as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+, and improved the accuracy of La-CQDs as an optical code both in vitro and in vivo.

The sizes of La-CQDs were characterized using TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements. The TEM results suggested that La-CQDs were mostly spherical dots and well separated from each other (Fig.2 A), with average size about 4.3±0.34nm (based on statistical analysis of more than 100 dots). In support of the TEM results, DLS results showed that the sizes of La-CQDs were 5.5±1.3nm (Fig. S2A). A well-resolved crystal lattice noted in the HRTEM image (inset in Fig.1A) confirmed the crystalline structure of the La-CQDs, with labeled interplanar distances of 0.32nm, consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of graphene. The XRD pattern of N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs (Fig. S3) demonstrated that the (002) interlayer spacing distance between N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs was 0.33nm (based on the calculation from 2dsinθ=nλ), which was similar to that of the bulk graphite, showing good crystallization characteristics.

The La-CQDs showed additional characteristic peaks corresponding to La 4d (104.5eV), La-3d5/2 (836.4eV) and La 3d3/2 (851.6eV) confirming the successful chelation of La3+ onto the surface of N,P-CQDs (Fig.2B). These results were consistent with the EDS measurement (Fig.2C). Elemental analysis (Fig.2C) showed the composition of La-CQDs as C 64.88, N 11.96, O 17.78, P 2.52, and La (calculated) 2.86wt%, indicating that they were phosphorus, nitrogen, and lanthanum triplex-doped CQDs. The survey X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) scan of La-CQDs, revealed that La-CQDs were mainly composed of carbon, phosphorus, nitrogen, oxygen, and lanthanum with the binding energy peaks at 531.1, 396.7, 285.4, 197.5, 131.3, 104.5, 836.4, and 851.6eV corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, P 2s, P 2p, La 4d, La 3d5/2, and La 3d3/2, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. S4A) exhibited five peaks at 284.3, 284.7, 285.4, 286.1, and 287.0eV, which were associated with C 1s states in CC, CC, COH/COC, CN, and CO, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S4B) which was deconvoluted into four peaks at 397.5, 398.2, 399.0, and 399.6eV, representing N 1s states in CNC, N(C)3 functionalities, pyridinic-like N, and pyrrolic-like N, respectively. The O 1s spectrum of the La-CQDs (Fig. S4C) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 531.3, 531.6, 533.4, and 535.6eV, which were contributed to OC, CO, CO/COC, and HO, respectively. The P 2p peak (Fig. S4D) depicted P 2p3/2 and PC binding motifs of La-CQDs.

FT-IR was used here to compare CQDs with ATP since it provided detailed chemical information. There were some difference between CQDs and ATP (Fig.2D). First, a strong and broad peak at 3390 cm-1 for CQDs indicated the existence of free OH or NH stretching vibration which was from hydroxyl or amine groups on the surface. The wide absorption peaks at 1300 cm-1 were attributed to stretching vibration of PO, indicated that P was successfully doped in CQDs. The strong peak of N,P-doped CQDs at 1677cm−1 may result from stretching vibration of CC and CO while the peak of CQDs at 1400cm−1 and 1600cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate anions, respectively. However, the disappearance of the absorption peaks at 1647 and 2392cm−1 suggests that ATP may be mostly carbonized during hydrothermal process. Compared with the spectrum of N,P-CQDs, the absorption bands of La-CQDs had a slight shift and exhibited increased peak intensity in the region of 1300–800cm−1. For example, the absorption bands of La-CQDs showed a shift to higher absorption around 3394cm−1 (OH/NH). Similarly, the zeta potential also proved that La3+ was successfully doped in N,P-CQDs (Fig. S2B, C). Compared with the zeta potentials of N,P-CQDs (−17.42mV), the zeta potentials of La-CQDs had increased (3.58mV).

The thermal properties of N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs were explored by TGA using a heating rate of 10°C/min up to 800°C in nitrogen atmosphere (Fig.3 A). Based on the TGA curve, the weight loss process of N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs can be divided roughly into two stages. In the initial stage of heating, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs had a significant weight loss at 25–350°C, which was mainly caused by the released CQDs bound water and pyrogas (NO, CO, CO2 and NO2) [39]. Compared with N,P-CQDs, the water adsorption ability of La-CQDs is decreased, due to the coordination of La3+ with ATP, which greatly weakens its ability to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. In the second stage between 350 and 800°C, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve rapidly declined which indicted that the incompletely carbonized segment of N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs changed carbon materials. The carbon residue of La-CQDs was 50%, which was increased by 8.2% compared with N,P-CQDs (41.8%). These changes indicated that a large number of La3+ through electrostatic attraction and coordination role into the ATP molecule. Therefore, the La-O coordination bond not only prevented the decomposition of oxygen-containing groups but also improved the thermal stability of the material.

The optical properties were measured by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The UV–vis spectra of N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs showed similar absorption profiles with two characteristic absorption peaks around 245 and 280nm (Fig.3B). The peaks are probably due to (π-π∗) transition of CC at the aromatic region. Compared with the fluorescent photographs of N,P-CQDs, La-CQDs aqueous solution showed luminous blue light that was taken under UV light (365nm) in the inset of Fig.3B. The PL spectrum clearly showed that there were significant emission wavelength shifts between N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs (Fig.3C). Correspondingly, La-CQDs and N,P-CQDs displayed an emission wavelength tunable feature that the excitation spectrum had shifted at different excitation wavelengths (Figs. 3D and S5A), and the fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of the prepared N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs was calculated to be 9.8% and 13.9%, respectively.

Almost no coagulation and bleaching were observed after uninterrupted exposure under UV lamp for 2h (Fig. S6A) and dissolved with 100 mM H2O2 (performed in pH 7.0, 10 mM PBS) for 30min (Fig. S6B), indicating La-CQDs fluorescence was considerable stable. We then investigated the fluorescence response of La-CQDs at different pHs (Fig.4 A). At lower and higher pH, La-CQDs showed low fluorescence intensity. Interestingly, when pH was at 4, La-CQDs had strongest fluorescence intensity. When pH was between 4 and 11, there was no significantly change in the fluorescence intensity for La-CQDs (Fig. S6C). This result may be caused by the phosphoric acid group and the amino group on the surface of the La-CQDs. The amino may be protonated in acidic solutions and phosphoric acid group may be deprotonated in alkaline. So we selected pH 7.0 for the following experiments. To further study the fluorescence stability of La-CQDs under high salt concentration, we dissolved La-CQDs in different concentrations of NaCl and recorded the response of the fluorescence (Fig. S6D). We found that the fluorescence intensity of La-CQDs did not decline significantly when the concentration of NaCl changed from 0 to 1.0mol/l, which means that La-CQDs can still play a part in a complex environment.

The following parameters were optimized: reaction time, mass of LaCl3·2H2O, and reaction temperature. The corresponding data and figures are given in the Electronic Supporting Material. We also found that there was obvious difference in the fluorescence intensity for La-CQDs prepared at 160°C (Fig. S7A, B). Then, we found that La-CQDs prepared at different time (4, 6, 8, and 10h) had no significant difference in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S7C, D). However, La-CQDs prepared at 8h were well dispersed with a narrow size distribution (Fig. S1). Hence, we chose 8h as the optimum reaction time. Then, we studied La-CQDs prepared at different mass of LaCl3·2H2O, and the fluorescence intensity of La-CQDs prepared from 1.5g LaCl3·2H2O was the highest (Fig. S7E, F). Therefore, 1.5g of LaCl3·2H2O was selected for the synthesis of La-CQDs synthesized.

There were so many carboxyl groups, phosphoric acid groups, and amino groups on the surface of La-CQDs. Therefore, we thought that the La-CQDs may be used to detect metal ions [40]. We further studied the fluorescence response of La-CQDs in the presence of different metal ions at the concentration of 25µM. It was noticeable that only Hg2+ induces a decrease in the fluorescence of La-CQDs, implying that La-CQDs can be used to detect Hg2+ selectively in an aqueous solution [27]. To determine the florescence detecting effect of the synthesized La-CQDs, quenching of the PL properties of the La-CQDs in the presence of Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Na+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, and Zn2+ ions were successfully determined. The PL spectra of N,P-CQDs were measured in the presence of 25µM different metal ions (Fig. S8A). The I0/I plots in the presence of 25µM different metal ions were shown in Fig.4B, where I and I0 indicated the PL intensity at 460nm in presence or absence of different metal ions. Remarkably, the emission spectra of La-CQDs did not show noticeable changes for any of these metal ions, indicating that there was no energy transfer between the metal ions and La-CQDs. PL intensity decreased slightly with the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions. With the addition of Hg2+ ions, nearly 50% of the fluorescence was quenched. However, the emission spectra of N,P-CQDs did not show noticeable changes for Hg2+ ions, suggesting that N,P-CQDs not provide a means for detecting metal ions selectively.

We then assessed the sensitivity of La-CQDs to Hg2+. There was a Hg2+ concentration-dependent quenching of the fluorescence of La-CQDs. Under the same conditions, different concentrations of Hg2+ were added into La-CQDs solution. The fluorescent intensity of La-CQDs at 460nm gradually decreased via increasing the Hg2+ concentration. A plot of I/I0 with different concentrations of Hg2+ and the decrease of PL intensity at 460nm in the presence of increasing concentrations of Hg2+ has been shown in the inset of Fig.4C. The detection limit for Hg2+ was approximately 0.1µM and there was a linear calibration plot in the range of 0.5 and 40µM with a correlation coefficient of 0.994 (Fig. S8B). Those results further revealed that the La-CQDs were highly selective to Hg2+, which has generated higher affinity with carboxyl groups, phosphoric acid groups, and amino groups on the surface of La-CQDs. Hg2+ induced-fluorescence quenching of La-CQDs is ascribed to the close proximity of La-CQDs induced by the chelation of Hg2+ with the carboxyl groups, phosphoric acid group, and amino group of La-CQDs, which changes the electronic structure of La-CQDs and affects the distribution of excitons, thus facilitating the non-radiative recombination of excitons through the charge transfer process and leading to the fluorescence quenching of La-CQDs. Therefore, the La-CQDs can be developed as bioprobes to detect Hg2+ in practical samples.

The time-resolved fluorescence measurements, which were carried out on a Time-resolved Fluorescence Life Time, were applied to further check the charge transfer and exciton recombination process of the La-CQDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+ ions. As shown in Fig.4D, the average fluorescence lifetime of pure La-CQDs is 4.65ns, obtained using a deconvolution technique by IBH DAS6 analysis software, reflecting a fast exciton recombination process. After the addition of Hg2+ ions, the lifetime of the La-CQDs is decreased to 2.68ns. The significantly reduction in fluorescence lifetime indicates that dynamic quenching occurred, and further confirms the fast electron transfer process in the La-CQDs-Hg2+ system.

The bioimaging property of La-CQDs was tested by confocal fluorescence microscope. The multi-color fluorescence of HepG2 cells in the presence of La-CQDs was observed in the Fig.5 A, B. Images captured using 2D cell culture are unable to confirm whether the fluorescent probes actually penetrated inside the cell or remained on the surface. To resolve this uncertainty, further tests were performed using 3D cell culture to examine the internalization of La-CQDs. From the 3D reconstruction, we observe that La-CQDs allows accurate 3D imaging of the HepG2 cells and that the La-CQDs had penetrated into the cytoplasm and the nucleus (Fig.5D, E). Thus, the 3D images confirm that the La-CQDs probes have been internalized by the cells rather than being bound or adsorbed on the surface of HepG2 cells. The La-CQDs can display different colors of fluorescence in the bioimaging of cells using different excitation wavelengths because the La-CQDs possessed a relatively wide excitation wavelength [31]. Fig. S9 showed that La-CQDs were readily taken up by E. coli and S. aureus, suggesting that the La-CQDs can be used to the E. coli and S. aureus. In a control experiment, no fluorescent signal is detected in E. coli and S. aureus in the absence of La-CQDs.

The potential use of La-CQDs as luminescent agents for in vivo imaging was investigated using female nude mice. Intravenous injection of La-CQDs was performed in the mice tail, and the images were taken on Maestro 2 Multispectral Small-Animal Imaging System. A small animal fluorescence imaging system was used to study the in vivo distribution of La-CQDs. As shown in Fig.5G, the pseudocolor images of the mice tail injected with La-CQDs exhibited bright emissions from La-CQDs, which was consistent with previous in vivo distribution on CQDs. In summary, the results reported here demonstrate that La-CQDs can serve as a new plateau of fluorescent bioimaging and show promise for delivering drugs delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular imaging of A549 cancer cells in the presence of La-CQDs only and in the presence of La-CQDs with Hg2+ metal ions has been shown in the Fig. 6 . Intense imaging of cells treated with only La-CQDs was observed in the green filter, while the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Hg2+ (25μM) metal ions is considerably decreased. This indicates that Hg2+ ions quench fluorescence effectively in cells. This result demonstrates that the synthesized carbon quantum dots have good biocompatibility and are suitable for the detection of metal ions in living cells.

The antimicrobial activities of the La-CQDs and N,P-CQDs were then tested against a range of pathogenic and non-pathogenic microorganisms, including Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus). To study the effect of La-CQDs concentration on the antibacterial activity, a series of concentrated La-CQDs complexes were incubated for 24h at 37°C. The O.D values were measured at 650nm (Fig. S10). Compared with N,P-CQDs, the antibacterial activity was enhanced with the increase of La-@CQDs concentration, which indicates that the bacterial growth is inhibited completely. Similarly, the antimicrobial activities of La-CQDs against S. aureus and E. coli were investigated by bacterial adhesion test. The shape and number of the microorganism on the surface of the samples were observed by scanning electron microscope. As shown in Fig. 7A, B, bacteria on the N,P-CQDs sample surface did not change so much, and the shape and size were nearly the same as the living bacteria. However, bacteria seen on pictures from La-CQDs group were wizened or even totally broken, which indicates that both S. aureus and E. coli were killed by the La-CQDs.

The bacterial inhibition was examined through the MTT assay after a suspension of E. coli and S. aureus bacteria was cultured on three materials for 24h, respectively [41]. Compared with N,P-CQDs, we found that La-CQDs had a significant inhibitory effect against E. coli (Fig.7 C) and S. aureus (Fig.7D). This result showed that La-CQDs complexes have an enhanced antibacterial effect, which depends on the La-CQDs concentration. The bacterial toxicity may be due to the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged La-CQDs and negatively charged bacteria. Overall charge of the bacteria is negative due to the presence of excessive carboxylic acid in the cell wall. Therefore, the positively charged La-CQDs are easily absorbed to the bacteria and are attached to the bacterial cells. Attached La-CQDs mechanically damaged the bacterial cell wall and penetrated into the cell. Once the cell wall is damaged all cell constituents leak and cause cell death. Therefore, the La-CQDs would be promising choices of wound dressing additives for the treatment of surface wounds and could potentially be used in antimicrobial packaging applications to preserve the quality of food products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and vivo cytotoxicity study of La-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity is an important factor that determines the safety of biomedical materials. In vitro cytotoxicity of the various samples including ATP, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs were investigated using NIH3T3 cells. Figs. 8 A and S11A, B showed the proliferation rates of cells incubated with different doses of ATP, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs for 24, 36, and 48h, respectively. The proliferation rates of cells did not change obviously with the increase of incubation time. Also, ATP and N,P-CQDs exhibited no cytotoxicity to NIH3T3 cells compared with blank control, which demonstrates its potential application as a medical material. Furthermore, annexin V-FITC/PI staining was applied to analyze the dose-dependent effect of ATP, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs on cell apoptosis (Fig.8B). After incubation with 2.5mg/ml of ATP, N,P-CQDs, and La-CQDs for 24h, the percentage of viable cells is 99.9 or 100%, indicating that ATP, N,P-CQDs, and La-CQDs at high concentration have no significant effect on the cell viability or inducing significant cell apoptosis and necrosis.

Hemolysis testing determines the solubility of the blood cells in contact with foreign materials and has been a powerful in vitro test to assess the hemolysis property of biomedical materials and products [42]. When blood cells are destroyed, hemoglobin is subsequently delivered from the blood cells. It is known that for the materials having potential applications as medical materials, a lower than 5%, hemolysis rate is required [43]. In view of this, hemolysis experiments were conducted to evaluate the blood compatibility of ATP, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs. Fig.8B showed the hemolysis test results. Compared with N,P-CQDs, there was no significant effect in the degree of hemolysis for La-CQDs. The hemolysis rate of ATP, N,P-CQDs and La-CQDs were all below 5%. The morphologies of RBCs incubated with different stents were studied by using an optical microscope. Fig. S11C,e showed the morphologies of RBCs after exposure to ATP, several RBCs displayed a certain degree of deformation and swelling but most RBCs appeared in a normal biconcave shape. When contacted with N,P-CQDs (Fig. S11C,c) and La-CQDs (Fig. S11C,d), the edge of the RBC membrane was not smooth and lost its normal biconcave shape. These results indicate that the La-CQDs are safe for blood-contact applications and suitable for wound treatment.

Body weight and behaviors of the treated mice were monitored to evaluate in vivo toxicities of La-CQDs on a daily basis. Treatment with La-CQDs does not induce any visible adverse reaction on the behavior of mice during the 30days to control levels (Fig.8D). Biosafety are one of the most important characteristics to be taken into consideration for medical applications. For biodistribution analysis, the concentrations of P in major organs of mice with La-CQDs injection were measured by ICP-MS after 1 and 30days (Fig.8E). The concentration of P in major organs of treated mice increases slightly after 1day. After that, the concentration of P of in major organs of treated mice starts to recover gradually until the 30th day to control levels. Finally, the pathological image of major organs are demonstrated by histology at 1, 10 and 30days. Heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were collected, sliced, and stained by H&E. As shown in Fig.8F, no apparent damages were detected in all organs, especially in the liver, spleen, and kidneys, during the entire experimental time period.

In conclusion, we synthesized La-CQDs with multiple-active functional groups, which can be used in the detection of Hg2+, bioimaging, and inhibition of bacteria. Although the purity of La-CQDs is not completely satisfactory, the following results of XPS and EDX confirmed that the La3+ had successfully doped to the N,P-CQDs. Fluorescence of La-CQDs is quenched by Hg2+, demonstrating their potential applications in real samples. The antibacterial results showed that La-CQDs complexes have an enhanced antibacterial effect as a function of La-CQDs concentration. The colorful light emitting La-CQDs with high photostability and biocompatibility that are prepared in this manner, particularly those with strong blue fluorescence, have promising applications in multicolor displays and biomedical probes. Hematological, biochemical, and pathological analyses demonstrate that La-CQDs do not give rise to the long-term inflammatory reactions and obvious damages in mice.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jiangsu six category outstanding talent (2012-NY-031), Jiangsu province science and technology support plan (BE2015367), the Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund CX(15)1016, JHB05-21 and Nanjing-321, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.07.166.
